summary:
The Teutonic Knights (Order of the Teutonic House of St. Mary in Jerusalem, founded 1190 AD) were a German military religious order that began as a hospital order during the [[event:2220|Third Crusade's]] siege of Acre, evolved into one of the most powerful Crusading military orders, and eventually became the dominant military-colonial power of northeastern Europe, establishing a theocratic state in Prussia and the Baltic that lasted for centuries. After the [[event:92|Crusades]] in the Holy Land ended, the Order redirected its energies to 'crusades' against the pagan Baltic peoples — the Prussians, Livonians, and Lithuanians — receiving papal authorization to convert them by force.

The conquest of Prussia (1226–1285) was one of medieval Europe's most systematic colonial enterprises: the native Prussian population was decimated, enslaved, or assimilated; German colonists were settled on their lands; and the Order constructed a network of castles and fortified towns (including Königsberg, modern Kaliningrad) that became the basis of German settlement in the eastern Baltic. The Order's state — the Teutonic State of Prussia — was efficiently governed, economically productive, and militarily formidable, attracting crusading visitors from across Europe who came for the seasonal 'Lithuanian crusades' as a fashionable martial expedition. The Order's power was broken at the [[event:3014|Battle of Grunwald]] (1410) by a Polish-Lithuanian army, after which it gradually declined. Its Prussian territories eventually became the secular Duchy of Prussia (1525) under Hohenzollern rule — the state from which the later Kingdom of Prussia and eventually the German Empire descended.
